instruction: How do you implement graceful shutdown in a Node.js server? Show proper signal handling, connection draining, and resource cleanup.
output:
<think>
Graceful shutdown is critical for production Node.js services. Without it, killing the process drops active connections, loses in-flight requests, and can corrupt data. The pattern is: listen for shutdown signals (SIGTERM from orchestrators, SIGINT from Ctrl+C), stop accepting new connections, wait for existing connections to finish (with a timeout), clean up resources (database pools, file handles, timers), then exit. The key challenge is tracking active connections. HTTP keep-alive connections persist after requests complete, so you need to track them and destroy idle ones immediately while letting active ones finish. You also need a hard timeout — if connections don't drain within a reasonable time, force-close them. In Kubernetes/Docker, SIGTERM is sent before SIGKILL (usually 30s grace period), so you must shut down within that window. Common mistakes: not handling SIGTERM (container gets SIGKILL), not closing the server (new connections keep arriving), not draining keep-alive connections.
</think>

```javascript
const http = require('http');
const { promisify } = require('util');

class GracefulServer {
  constructor(handler) {
    this.server = http.createServer(handler);
    this.connections = new Set();
    this.isShuttingDown = false;
    this.shutdownTimeout = 30_000; // 30s grace period

    // Track all connections
    this.server.on('connection', (socket) => {
      this.connections.add(socket);
      socket.once('close', () => this.connections.delete(socket));
    });
  }

  listen(port) {
    return new Promise((resolve) => {
      this.server.listen(port, () => {
        console.log(`Server listening on port ${port}`);
        this.setupSignalHandlers();
        resolve();
      });
    });
  }

  setupSignalHandlers() {
    const shutdown = (signal) => {
      console.log(`Received ${signal}, starting graceful shutdown...`);
      this.shutdown();
    };

    process.on('SIGTERM', () => shutdown('SIGTERM')); // Docker/K8s stop
    process.on('SIGINT', () => shutdown('SIGINT'));    // Ctrl+C
  }

  async shutdown() {
    if (this.isShuttingDown) return;
    this.isShuttingDown = true;

    // 1. Stop accepting new connections
    console.log('Closing server to new connections...');
    const closeServer = promisify(this.server.close.bind(this.server));

    // 2. Set hard timeout
    const forceExit = setTimeout(() => {
      console.error(`Force shutdown after ${this.shutdownTimeout}ms`);
      process.exit(1);
    }, this.shutdownTimeout);
    forceExit.unref(); // Don't keep process alive just for this timer

    // 3. Close idle keep-alive connections immediately
    for (const socket of this.connections) {
      if (!socket._httpMessage) {
        // No active request — safe to destroy
        socket.destroy();
      } else {
        // Active request — set Connection: close so it closes after response
        socket.setHeader?.('Connection', 'close');
      }
    }

    try {
      // 4. Wait for active requests to complete
      await closeServer();
      console.log('All connections drained');

      // 5. Clean up resources
      await this.cleanup();

      console.log('Shutdown complete');
      process.exit(0);
    } catch (err) {
      console.error('Shutdown error:', err);
      process.exit(1);
    }
  }

  async cleanup() {
    // Close database pools, flush logs, etc.
    console.log('Cleaning up resources...');
    // await db.end();
    // await cache.quit();
    // await logger.flush();
  }
}

// === USAGE ===

const app = new GracefulServer((req, res) => {
  if (req.url === '/health') {
    // Health check returns 503 during shutdown
    if (app.isShuttingDown) {
      res.writeHead(503);
      res.end('Shutting down');
      return;
    }
    res.writeHead(200);
    res.end('OK');
    return;
  }

  // Simulate slow request
  setTimeout(() => {
    res.writeHead(200, { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' });
    res.end(JSON.stringify({ message: 'Hello' }));
  }, 1000);
});

app.listen(3000);
```

instruction: Explain Node.js stream backpressure. What causes it, how do you detect it, and how do you handle it properly?
output:
<think>
Backpressure occurs when a writable stream can't consume data as fast as a readable stream produces it. Without handling it, data accumulates in memory buffers and can cause the process to run out of memory. The Node.js stream API has built-in backpressure signaling: writable.write() returns false when the internal buffer exceeds highWaterMark, signaling the producer to pause. When the buffer drains, the 'drain' event fires, signaling the producer to resume. pipe() handles this automatically — it pauses the readable when the writable signals backpressure and resumes on drain. But when you manually read/write without pipe, you must handle backpressure yourself. The common mistake is ignoring the return value of write() and just pumping data as fast as possible. This works for small data but explodes memory with large files or fast producers. The pipeline() utility (util.promisify + stream.pipeline) is the modern way to connect streams with automatic backpressure and error handling.
</think>

```javascript
const { createReadStream, createWriteStream } = require('fs');
const { pipeline, Transform } = require('stream');
const { promisify } = require('util');
const pipelineAsync = promisify(pipeline);

// === THE PROBLEM: Ignoring backpressure ===

async function badCopy(src, dst) {
  const readable = createReadStream(src);
  const writable = createWriteStream(dst);

  readable.on('data', (chunk) => {
    // ❌ WRONG: Ignoring write() return value
    // If writable is slow, data accumulates in memory → OOM
    writable.write(chunk);
  });
}

// === MANUAL BACKPRESSURE HANDLING ===

async function manualCopy(src, dst) {
  const readable = createReadStream(src, { highWaterMark: 64 * 1024 });
  const writable = createWriteStream(dst, { highWaterMark: 64 * 1024 });

  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    readable.on('data', (chunk) => {
      const canContinue = writable.write(chunk);

      if (!canContinue) {
        // ✅ Backpressure detected — pause the readable
        readable.pause();

        // Resume when writable buffer drains
        writable.once('drain', () => {
          readable.resume();
        });
      }
    });

    readable.on('end', () => {
      writable.end();
    });
    writable.on('finish', resolve);
    readable.on('error', reject);
    writable.on('error', reject);
  });
}

// === BEST: Use pipeline() — handles everything automatically ===

async function bestCopy(src, dst) {
  await pipelineAsync(
    createReadStream(src),
    createWriteStream(dst)
  );
  // ✅ Backpressure handled automatically
  // ✅ Errors propagated and streams cleaned up
  // ✅ All streams destroyed on error (no resource leaks)
}

// === TRANSFORM STREAM with backpressure ===

class CompressTransform extends Transform {
  constructor() {
    super({ highWaterMark: 16 * 1024 }); // 16KB buffer
  }

  _transform(chunk, encoding, callback) {
    // Process chunk
    const processed = chunk.toString().toUpperCase();

    // Push to readable side — returns false if buffer full
    // But using callback is simpler: callback(null, data)
    callback(null, Buffer.from(processed));
  }

  _flush(callback) {
    // Called when all input is consumed
    callback();
  }
}

// Pipeline with transform — backpressure flows through entire chain
async function processFile(input, output) {
  await pipelineAsync(
    createReadStream(input),
    new CompressTransform(),
    createWriteStream(output)
  );
}

// === MONITORING BACKPRESSURE ===

function monitoredPipeline(readable, writable) {
  let backpressureEvents = 0;

  readable.on('data', (chunk) => {
    if (!writable.write(chunk)) {
      backpressureEvents++;
      readable.pause();
      console.log(`Backpressure #${backpressureEvents}, ` +
        `writable buffer: ${writable.writableLength} bytes`);
    }
  });

  writable.on('drain', () => {
    readable.resume();
  });

  writable.on('finish', () => {
    console.log(`Done. Total backpressure events: ${backpressureEvents}`);
  });
}
```

instruction: How do worker threads work in Node.js? When should you use them instead of child processes or the cluster module?
output:
<think>
Node.js worker threads (worker_threads module) run JavaScript in parallel OS threads within the same process. They share memory via SharedArrayBuffer and transfer data via structured clone (postMessage) or transferable objects. This is different from child_process.fork() which creates a new V8 instance in a new process with its own memory space. Worker threads are better for CPU-intensive JavaScript computation (parsing, encryption, image processing, ML inference) because they share the process memory space and can transfer data efficiently with zero-copy via SharedArrayBuffer. Child processes are better for isolation (crashes don't affect the main process) and for running non-JS programs. The cluster module is specifically for scaling HTTP servers across CPU cores — each worker handles requests independently. Key considerations: worker threads share the event loop's thread pool, so I/O-heavy workers compete with the main thread's I/O. Workers can't share non-transferable objects (functions, closures). The overhead of creating a worker is higher than a setTimeout but much lower than spawning a process.
</think>

```javascript
const {
  Worker, isMainThread, parentPort, workerData,
  MessageChannel, SHARE_ENV
} = require('worker_threads');
const os = require('os');

// === BASIC WORKER: CPU-intensive task ===

if (isMainThread) {
  // Main thread — spawn workers for heavy computation

  function runWorker(data) {
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      const worker = new Worker(__filename, { workerData: data });

      worker.on('message', resolve);
      worker.on('error', reject);
      worker.on('exit', (code) => {
        if (code !== 0) {
          reject(new Error(`Worker exited with code ${code}`));
        }
      });
    });
  }

  async function main() {
    // Parallel CPU work across threads
    const tasks = [
      runWorker({ type: 'fibonacci', n: 40 }),
      runWorker({ type: 'fibonacci', n: 38 }),
      runWorker({ type: 'fibonacci', n: 42 }),
    ];

    console.time('parallel');
    const results = await Promise.all(tasks);
    console.timeEnd('parallel');  // ~2s (parallel)
    console.log('Results:', results);

    // Compare: sequential would take ~6s
  }

  main().catch(console.error);

} else {
  // Worker thread — receives workerData, sends result back
  const { type, n } = workerData;

  if (type === 'fibonacci') {
    function fibonacci(n) {
      if (n <= 1) return n;
      return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2);
    }
    parentPort.postMessage(fibonacci(n));
  }
}

// === WORKER POOL: Reuse workers for efficiency ===

class WorkerPool {
  constructor(workerFile, size = os.cpus().length) {
    this.workers = [];
    this.queue = [];
    this.activeWorkers = new Map();

    for (let i = 0; i < size; i++) {
      const worker = new Worker(workerFile);
      worker.on('message', (result) => {
        const { resolve } = this.activeWorkers.get(worker);
        this.activeWorkers.delete(worker);
        resolve(result);
        this.processQueue();
      });
      worker.on('error', (err) => {
        const pending = this.activeWorkers.get(worker);
        if (pending) {
          pending.reject(err);
          this.activeWorkers.delete(worker);
        }
      });
      this.workers.push(worker);
    }
  }

  exec(data) {
    return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
      this.queue.push({ data, resolve, reject });
      this.processQueue();
    });
  }

  processQueue() {
    for (const worker of this.workers) {
      if (this.queue.length === 0) break;
      if (this.activeWorkers.has(worker)) continue;

      const task = this.queue.shift();
      this.activeWorkers.set(worker, task);
      worker.postMessage(task.data);
    }
  }

  async destroy() {
    await Promise.all(this.workers.map(w => w.terminate()));
  }
}

// === SHARED MEMORY: Zero-copy data sharing ===

function sharedMemoryExample() {
  // SharedArrayBuffer: both threads see same memory
  const shared = new SharedArrayBuffer(4 * 1024); // 4KB shared
  const view = new Int32Array(shared);

  const worker = new Worker(`
    const { parentPort, workerData } = require('worker_threads');
    const view = new Int32Array(workerData.shared);
    // Worker writes directly to shared memory
    Atomics.store(view, 0, 42);
    Atomics.notify(view, 0);  // Notify main thread
    parentPort.postMessage('done');
  `, { eval: true, workerData: { shared } });

  worker.on('message', () => {
    // Read from shared memory — no copying!
    console.log('Shared value:', Atomics.load(view, 0)); // 42
  });
}

// WHEN TO USE:
// worker_threads: CPU-intensive JS (parsing, crypto, ML), shared memory
// child_process:  isolation needed, running external programs, crashes ok
// cluster:        scaling HTTP servers across CPU cores
```

instruction: Show how to use the EventEmitter pattern effectively in Node.js. What are the best practices and common pitfalls?
output:
<think>
EventEmitter is the foundation of Node.js's event-driven architecture. It's used by HTTP servers, streams, file watchers, and many libraries. Best practices include: always handle 'error' events (unhandled error events crash the process), use once() for one-time listeners to prevent leaks, remove listeners when components are destroyed, and set maxListeners appropriately. Common pitfalls: memory leaks from never-removed listeners (Node warns at 11 listeners by default), async errors in listeners that become unhandled rejections, listener ordering dependencies (listeners fire in registration order, which is fragile), and the synchronous nature of emit() (all listeners run synchronously before emit returns, which can block). For typed events, extending EventEmitter with TypeScript generics provides compile-time safety. The captureRejections option (Node 12+) converts rejected promises in async listeners to error events automatically.
</think>

```javascript
const { EventEmitter } = require('events');

// === BASIC: Typed event emitter ===

class OrderProcessor extends EventEmitter {
  constructor() {
    super({ captureRejections: true }); // Auto-handle async errors
  }

  async processOrder(order) {
    try {
      this.emit('order:received', order);

      const validated = await this.validate(order);
      this.emit('order:validated', validated);

      const charged = await this.charge(validated);
      this.emit('order:charged', charged);

      this.emit('order:complete', charged);
      return charged;
    } catch (err) {
      this.emit('error', err); // MUST be handled or process crashes!
      throw err;
    }
  }

  async validate(order) {
    if (!order.items?.length) throw new Error('Empty order');
    return { ...order, validated: true };
  }

  async charge(order) {
    return { ...order, charged: true, chargeId: 'ch_' + Date.now() };
  }
}

const processor = new OrderProcessor();

// CRITICAL: Always handle 'error' events
processor.on('error', (err) => {
  console.error('Order error:', err.message);
});

// Logging listener
processor.on('order:received', (order) => {
  console.log(`Order received: ${order.id}`);
});

// Metrics listener
processor.on('order:complete', (order) => {
  console.log(`Order complete: ${order.id}, charge: ${order.chargeId}`);
});

// One-time listener — auto-removes after firing once
processor.once('order:complete', () => {
  console.log('First order processed!');
});

// === PITFALL 1: Memory leak from unremoved listeners ===

class Dashboard {
  constructor(processor) {
    // ❌ BAD: If Dashboard is created many times, listeners accumulate
    // processor.on('order:complete', this.onComplete);

    // ✅ GOOD: Save reference for cleanup
    this.handler = (order) => this.onComplete(order);
    processor.on('order:complete', this.handler);
    this.processor = processor;
  }

  onComplete(order) {
    console.log('Dashboard update:', order.id);
  }

  destroy() {
    // ✅ Remove listener on cleanup
    this.processor.removeListener('order:complete', this.handler);
  }
}

// === PITFALL 2: Emit is synchronous! ===

const emitter = new EventEmitter();

emitter.on('data', (val) => {
  // This runs SYNCHRONOUSLY during emit()
  // Heavy work here blocks the event loop!
  console.log('Listener 1:', val);
});

emitter.on('data', (val) => {
  console.log('Listener 2:', val);
});

console.log('Before emit');
emitter.emit('data', 42);
console.log('After emit');
// Output: Before emit → Listener 1: 42 → Listener 2: 42 → After emit
// All listeners complete before emit() returns!

// === BEST PRACTICE: AbortController for cleanup ===

function monitorWithAbort(emitter, signal) {
  const handler = (data) => console.log('Data:', data);
  emitter.on('data', handler);

  // Automatic cleanup when aborted
  signal.addEventListener('abort', () => {
    emitter.removeListener('data', handler);
    console.log('Monitoring stopped');
  });
}

const ac = new AbortController();
monitorWithAbort(emitter, ac.signal);
// Later: ac.abort() — cleanly removes listener

// === BEST PRACTICE: events.on() for async iteration ===

const { on } = require('events');

async function processEvents(emitter) {
  // Modern async iteration over events
  const ac = new AbortController();

  setTimeout(() => ac.abort(), 5000); // Stop after 5s

  try {
    for await (const [data] of on(emitter, 'data', { signal: ac.signal })) {
      console.log('Async event:', data);
    }
  } catch (err) {
    if (err.code !== 'ABORT_ERR') throw err;
  }
}
```
